attributes.stem:
A 41-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of mild fever, malaise, and severe pain, redness, and swelling of his right index finger. He works as a dentist and has a history of cold sores. He has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His right index finger is erythematous and tender, with grouped vesicles on an erythematous base around the nail fold. The affected area is warm to touch, with clear discharge from a broken vesicle, but there is no fluctuance.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of herpetic whitlow, a herpes simplex virus infection of the finger. The history of being a healthcare worker (dentist) exposed to oral fluid and having cold sores increases the risk. The characteristic grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, along with severe pain, are typical features and usually requires only clinical diagnosis. If confirmation is needed, Tzanck test and culture can be performed.

Herpetic whitlow is caused by either HSV-1 or HSV-2, with HSV-1 being more common in healthcare workers due to exposure to oral secretions. It is spread by direct contact from oral or genital herpes, and can reactivate intermittently.

Paronychia is a bacterial infection of the nail fold that typically presents with pain, erythema, and swelling around the nail. However, it usually develops after trauma or excessive moisture exposure and does not present with grouped vesicles.

Felon is a painful infection of the pulp space of the fingertip that typically presents with tense swelling and severe throbbing pain. It usually occurs after penetrating trauma and does not present with vesicles as well.

Cellulitis typically presents with spreading erythema, warmth, and swelling but without the characteristic vesicular lesions seen in this case. It also tends to have more diffuse involvement rather than being localised to the digit.

Pyogenic granuloma is a benign vascular lesion that presents as a rapidly growing, friable nodule that bleeds easily. It does not present with vesicles or significant pain.

Management of herpetic whitlow is primarily supportive, including oral antivirals if presenting within 48 hours of symptom onset and covering the area to prevent spread. Importantly, incision and drainage should be avoided as it may delay healing and increase viral spread. The condition is usually self-limiting and resolves within 2-4 weeks. If there is multiple flares or immunosupression, patients may consider prophylactic antiviral treatment.
learningPoints: Herpetic whitlow typically presents as painful vesicles on fingers.
The diagnosis of herpetic whitlow is primarily clinical, based on characteristic appearance and history.
references: Wong V, Couslson I.  Herpetic whitlow.  DermNet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/herpetic-whitlow
Ismail A, Gold W. Herpetic Whitlow in a 39-year-old woman.  CAnadian Medical Association Journal. 2020: 192 (35).  Available at: https://www.cmaj.ca/content/192/35/e1010
